concepts.title: ZPLS Node Group
concepts.summary: A collection of up to 20 ZPLS modules that work together to support up to 100,000 users. Node groups enable horizontal scaling of survivability capacity and support optional redundancy for high availability. Designers should understand that node groups abstract away individual module management, presenting a unified survivability unit to the admin.
key_points: Supports up to 20 ZPLS modules in a single group
Capacity scales: supports up to 100,000 users without redundancy, 50,000 with redundancy
Users and common areas automatically distributed across nodes via round-robin
Only unassigned modules (not tied to a site or other group) can be added

concepts.title: Node Group Redundancy
concepts.summary: An optional high-availability configuration within node groups where backup nodes automatically take over if primary nodes fail. Redundancy requires a strict 2:1 primary-to-backup ratio. When enabled, total user capacity is halved (from 100K to 50K with 20 modules). This trade-off between capacity and resilience must be clearly communicated in the admin interface.
key_points: 2:1 primary-to-backup node ratio required for proper redundancy
Halves total user capacity when enabled (100K to 50K with 20 modules)
Backup nodes are selected from the node list after adding primary nodes

concepts.title: Round-Robin User Distribution
concepts.summary: The automated mechanism by which the Zoom cloud assigns users and common areas across ZPLS modules within a node group. Distribution is balanced across all nodes in the group and cannot be manually controlled or modified by administrators. This is a significant UX implication -- admins have no control over which users go to which nodes, so the interface should focus on aggregate group health rather than per-node user details.
key_points: Automatic balanced user distribution across all nodes in the group
Cannot be manually controlled or modified by administrators
Managed entirely by Zoom cloud
Ensures optimal balance without admin intervention

concepts.title: Zoom Client ZPLS Validation
concepts.summary: The end-user-facing verification that ZPLS configuration has been successfully pushed to the Zoom desktop client. The client shows a Configured status upon initial setup and transitions to Registered once the client connects to ZPLS. This is the only end-user-visible aspect of ZPLS, making it critical that designers provide clear, reassuring feedback.
key_points: Status transitions: Configured -> Registered upon successful ZPLS connection
Viewable in: Settings > Statistics > Phone tab
Displays ZPLS IP address for user verification
Recommended to test with simulated failover after validation

user_roles.title: Account Owner / Administrator
user_roles.summary: Configures node groups, assigns them to sites and route groups, and creates the overall survivability topology. This role manages the structural configuration only -- they cannot control individual user-to-node assignments. Designers should make this limitation clear in the interface to set appropriate expectations.
key_points: Creates node groups, enables redundancy, adds modules
Assigns groups to sites and route groups
Cannot manually control user distribution (automated round-robin)
Requires account owner or admin privileges with edit access

user_roles.title: End User (Zoom Client User)
user_roles.summary: The only role that can validate ZPLS configuration on the local client. While admins configure the infrastructure, end users verify it works on their device. This validation step is currently buried in Statistics > Phone tab, which is not an intuitive location for non-technical users.
key_points: Validates ZPLS configuration on local Zoom client
Accesses Settings > Statistics > Phone tab to check status
Status indicators: Configured (setup complete) and Registered (connected)

task_flows.title: Create ZPLS Node Group
task_flows.summary: A form-based creation workflow where admins name the group, optionally add a description, enable redundancy, and add ZPLS modules. The interface lists only unassigned modules, preventing configuration errors. When redundancy is enabled, backup node selection appears with the 2:1 ratio requirement. This is a multi-step setup where later steps depend on earlier selections.
key_points: Navigate: Phone System Management > Company Info > Account Settings > Zoom Node > Local Survivability > Manage > Node groups tab > New
Enter Group Name, optional Description, enable Redundancy toggle
Add up to 20 unassigned ZPLS modules
If redundancy enabled, select backup nodes at 2:1 ratio

task_flows.title: Assign Node Group to Site and Route Group
task_flows.summary: An assignment workflow that links a node group to a specific Site and a pre-configured Survivability Route Group. The edit interface uses a modal window, and the assignment triggers automatic round-robin user distribution. The admin cannot control which users land on which nodes after assignment.
key_points: Navigate: Phone System Management > Company Info > Account Settings > Zoom Node > Local Survivability > Manage > Node groups tab
Click '...' (More), Edit, select Site and Route Group
Assignment triggers automatic round-robin user distribution
Route Group must be pre-created (dependency on KB0085804)

task_flows.title: Validate ZPLS Configuration on Zoom Client
task_flows.summary: An end-user validation workflow where users check the Zoom client to confirm ZPLS is configured correctly. The status starts as Configured and changes to Registered upon successful connection. This flow is located in the Statistics > Phone tab, which is a technical/debug location rather than a user-facing status area. Designers should consider surfacing ZPLS status in a more visible location.
key_points: User logs into Zoom client, opens profile picture menu > Settings > Statistics > Phone tab
Verifies Local Survivability status shows Configured (initial) or Registered (connected)
Confirms displayed IP address is correct
Recommended follow-up: simulated failover testing

constraints.title: Node Group Module Assignment Restrictions
constraints.summary: Only ZPLS modules that are currently unassigned (not tied to a site or other node group) can be added to a node group. This prevents configuration conflicts but means admins must first unassign a module if it was previously assigned elsewhere. The interface should clearly indicate why certain modules are unavailable for selection.
key_points: Only unassigned ZPLS modules can be added to a node group
Modules already assigned to a Site or another node group are excluded
Maximum 20 modules per node group

constraints.title: Redundancy Ratio Requirement
constraints.summary: When redundancy is enabled, backup nodes must be configured at a 2:1 ratio with primary nodes (2 primary nodes per 1 backup node). This hard ratio constraint means the admin interface must validate selections and prevent saving if the ratio is incorrect. Additionally, enabling redundancy halves the total user capacity.
key_points: 2 primary nodes : 1 backup node ratio required
Enabling redundancy halves total user capacity
Interface must validate ratio before allowing save

constraints.title: Automated Distribution Limitation
constraints.summary: User and common area distribution across ZPLS nodes is fully automated via round-robin and cannot be manually controlled or modified. This means the admin interface should not expose per-node user assignment controls, as they would have no effect. This is a significant design constraint that simplifies the UI but removes admin flexibility.
key_points: User distribution is fully automated, no manual control
Admins cannot specify which users go to which nodes
Interface should focus on aggregate group health rather than per-node details

constraints.title: ZPLS Prerequisites for Node Groups
constraints.summary: Node groups require Business/Education/Enterprise account, Zoom Node and Zoom Phone subscriptions, a deployed ZPLS service, ZPLS enabled, and a pre-configured SBC with ZPLS. This creates a dependency chain where node groups are one of the last things configured in the ZPLS setup sequence.
key_points: Business, Education, or Enterprise account required
Zoom Node and Zoom Phone subscriptions required
ZPLS service must be deployed and enabled
SBC must be pre-configured with ZPLS (dependency on KB0085804)

ux_patterns.title: Local Survivability Management Hub Pattern
ux_patterns.summary: The Local Survivability Manage screen serves as a hub with multiple tabs (including Node groups) for configuring different aspects of ZPLS. This tab-based organization groups related survivability configuration together while keeping each concern in its own tab. The 'New' button and '...' (More) menu on rows provide consistent CRUD interaction patterns.
key_points: Local Survivability > Manage screen as central hub for ZPLS configuration
Tab-based organization (Node groups tab alongside other ZPLS config tabs)
New button for creation, '...' (More) menu for row-level edit actions
Modal windows for edit operations

ux_patterns.title: Client-Side Status Validation Pattern
ux_patterns.summary: ZPLS configuration validation is pushed to the end-user client rather than being verified solely in the admin portal. The Zoom client's Statistics > Phone tab shows Configuration status (Configured/Registered) and IP address. This pattern places validation responsibility on end users, which suggests the need for guided validation instructions or an admin-triggered validation check.
key_points: Validation occurs on client side, not admin portal side
Statistics > Phone tab is a technical/debug location, not user-friendly
Status transition from Configured to Registered provides clear user feedback
Recommended simulated failover test after validation
